joke.raw_joke: My wife think it is weird that I stare through the window during a heavy rainstorm. — It would be a lot less weird if she would just let me in

blocks.content:
### seed-083

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-083
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-083-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Production Duration Flag:** Target was 9.2s. Actual output is **11.31s** — 2.11 seconds over brief. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 8
- punchline_clarity: 8
- caption_readability: 8
- audio_comfort: 6
- shareability: 7

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 8
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 54/70

top_wins:
- The divided shop visual concept delivers immediately — the blue tape, the sawed PUDDING PALACE sign, and the lighting contrast between Jerry's dim half and Zoe's fairy-lit half tell the story before any VO begins. This is the hook working correctly.
- Jerry's resignation and Zoe's delight are the right emotional contrast. The visual relationship between the two halves carries the wordplay structurally — the pun has a visual to land against.
- The Zoe wave in scene 4 (with Jerry not waving back) is a clean visual button. It's the right ending.
- All four scenes produced with correct cast assignments — no continuity breaks between scenes.

critical_issues: None blocking publish. Duration overrun requires trim before final delivery.

micro_edits:
- **Duration trim (required):** Video runs 11.31s against a 9.2s brief — approximately 2.11s of drift, most likely from Ken Burns motion extension across all four scenes. Trim each scene to brief targets (Scene 01: 2.0s, Scene 02: 3.6s, Scene 03: max 0.3s, Scene 04: 3.1s). This is an edit-pass cut, not a regeneration. Target final runtime: 9.0–9.4s.
- **Scene 3 duration:** The sigh beat was specified at 0.2–0.3s maximum. If it's running longer than 0.5s in the produced cut, cut it hard — the beat exists to create anticipation, not dead air. Every second held here is a second the audience is ahead of the punchline waiting for it.
- **Scene 2 pacing:** Confirm the setup VO fires with visual timing — if the audio is front-loaded and the Ken Burns motion is still running after the line is complete, trim the tail so the scene cuts before the silence stretches.

gate_decision: CONDITIONAL_PASS
next_status: ready_to_publish
recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: 7.8

lena_final_note: The shop looks incredible. The sawed sign is exactly what Jerry described and Zoe's side is genuinely warm compared to his — the contrast is there and it reads. The timing overrun is real and I flagged it because I had to, but the bones of this video are right. Noah, I know you're going to call it correctly. Just... the visual on this one is SO good. I just needed you to know that before the scores.

noah_final_note: seed-083: CONDITIONAL_PASS. 54/70. Duration overrun is the sole blocking item — 11.31s against a 9.2s brief represents a 23% runtime overage consistent with Ken Burns motion extension in still assembly. Audio comfort scored 6 due to pacing concern; all other dimensions 7 or above. The trim is a single editing pass. Visual continuity clean across all four scenes. Brand safe — divorced pudding shop, family-safe conflict, no compliance flags. Advancing to ready_to_publish pending trim confirmation. Lena's read on the visual quality is accurate.

---

### seed-089

**QA_REVIEW_v1**

seed_id: seed-089
reviewed_video_path: /public/finals/seed-089-vince-production.mp4

**⚠️ Critical Production Duration Flag:** Target was 8.8s. Actual output is **6.04s** — 2.76 seconds under brief. This is a 31% compression. Flagged before scoring begins.

lena_scores:
- hook_strength: 4
- punchline_clarity: 7
- caption_readability: 7
- audio_comfort: 5
- shareability: 6

noah_scores:
- visual_continuity: 7
- brand_safety: 9

total_score: 45/70

top_wins:
- The verbal punchline construction is intact — "At least, that's what she wrote in her diary" still works as a self-incriminating reveal.
- Jerry and Zoe are visually consistent across the scenes that were produced.

critical_issues:
- **Cold open compressed to non-functional duration.** Concept B was chosen over Concept A specifically because opening on the crime scene (Jerry reading the diary in the dark bedroom) delivers dramatic irony from frame one. At 6.04s total for a four-scene video with 5.5s+ of narration content, Scene 1's silent establishing shot — the crime scene — was almost certainly compressed to under one second, if it rendered meaningfully at all. Scene 1 was budgeted at 2.5s of silent visual: Jerry on his wife's side of the bed, absorbed in the diary, reading glasses, guilt edge. This visual IS the concept. Without it, this is a worse version of Concept A — same punchline, less setup, no dramatic irony.
- **Pacing failure:** At 6.04s, even the audio delivery will feel rushed. The 0.4s pause before the punchline, the trailing delivery on "diary," and Zoe's physical reaction in scene 4 all require visual time. A 6.04s runtime cannot accommodate four scenes of meaningful duration.

micro_edits: N/A — not fixable in a single editing pass. The production timing is structurally wrong. This requires Vince to reassemble with correct scene durations.

gate_decision: FAIL
next_status: queued_for_scene_production

**Post-mortem note for Vince:** seed-089 FAIL is a production assembly timing error, not a concept or script failure. The joke is strong. Concept B is the correct choice. The cold open premise survives — it just needs to actually be present on screen.

Return instructions:
- Scene 01 (crime scene): must run **minimum 2.0s**, ideally 2.5s. Silent. No VO. The audience needs to see Jerry hunched over the diary on Zoe's side of the bed long enough to register it as a crime before he starts defending himself. This is the entire value proposition of Concept B.
- Scene 02 (hallway defense setup): 2.7s with VO for the setup line.
- Scene 03 (beat): 0.3–0.4s maximum.
- Scene 04 (punchline + Zoe reaction): 3.0–3.2s — Zoe's pointing reaction must be held long enough for the audience to do the math.
- Target total: 8.5–9.0s.

recommended_thumbnail_moment_sec: N/A — not advancing in this state. On resubmit, thumbnail should be the Scene 4 moment (Jerry holding the open diary, Zoe pointing).

lena_final_note: I'm not going to pretend this one is okay, because it's not. The cold open was the whole reason Jerry picked Concept B over Concept A. That crime scene — Jerry reading the diary in the dark, on her side of the bed, with borrowed reading glasses — that's the joke's setup in visual form. At 6.04 seconds, it's gone. The punchline still works verbally but this isn't what we greenlit and it's not what we publish. Noah... I trust your call. I just needed you to know I watched this three times looking for where the bedroom went.

noah_final_note: seed-089: FAIL. 45/70. Hook strength scored 4 — a critical dimension below threshold. Audio comfort scored 5 — below threshold. Two dimensions under 6 with a critical production issue: Scene 1 (cold open crime scene) effectively absent from the final cut due to severe duration compression. 6.04s actual against an 8.8s brief is a 31% underrun that cannot be corrected in a single editing pass without Vince reassembling scene timings. The concept is sound. The execution is not. Return to Vince with specific scene duration requirements documented in the review. Lena's identification of the missing cold open is correct. That is the critical failure point.

---

### seed-106

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Husband / Narrator (locked outside) | Noah Check — Dad persona (adapted: Man Standing in Rain with a Completely Reasonable Explanation) | The joke only works if the narrator delivers it with absolute sincerity and no awareness that he is the weird one. Noah's "rational foil / compliance comedy" archetype is built for this — he has assessed the situation, identified the logical solution (being let in), and is explaining why his wife's perception is technically incorrect. He is soaking wet. He sees no problem with his analysis. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Noah: Casual home wardrobe — whatever he was wearing when this started. Damp hair. Possibly holding an umbrella that is not fully deployed (he grabbed it but it didn't help). No wedding ring removal needed — married energy is appropriate and visible.
- The wife is not on screen. She exists as a silhouette or warm light visible through the window behind Noah. If the shot concept includes the window, her shadow/outline is the visual anchor for "the person who could resolve this situation."
- Shot framing: Exterior shot, rain visible. Noah faces camera. The window and interior light are visible behind him. He is wet. He is calm.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Noah to camera, standing in rain, mild exasperation. Gestures slightly toward the house behind him. His tone is factual: he is not angry at his wife, he is presenting data about the situation's weirdness.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Noah. No gesture. No pointing. He states the obvious. His expression is genuine problem-solving — he has identified the fix and is sharing it. The audience is ahead of him. He is not.

---

### seed-106

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-106
concepts_count: 2
Milo script: MISSING from payload (using raw joke). Working script: Setup: "My wife thinks it's weird that I stare through the window during a rainstorm." Punchline: "It would be less weird if she'd just let me in."
Riley casting honored: Noah Check (Dad persona) solo. Wife not on screen — exists as silhouette/warm light through window. Per Riley: Noah is soaking wet, sees no problem with his analysis.

---

**Concept A — "The Rain Report"**
Style: Exterior shot, Noah standing in rain outside his own house, explaining the situation
Duration: 8.6s — Clean two-beat, the visual does heavy lifting.

Master video prompt:
"Exterior of a suburban house at night in heavy rain, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Noah Check (short cropped hair, rectangular glasses, polo shirt — soaking wet, rain-darkened fabric, hair plastered to forehead) standing on the front step or porch, facing camera. Behind him: a lit window showing the warm interior glow of the living room, a vague silhouette of a woman visible through the glass. Rain falls heavily. Puddles on the walkway. Noah is wet. Noah is calm. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.2s): HOOK — The Man in the Rain
The exterior of a suburban house at night. Heavy rain falls in visible sheets, lit from above by a porch light. Puddles form on the concrete walkway. Standing on the front step, just outside the front door, is Noah Check — a sturdy man with short cropped hair (now plastered to his forehead), rectangular glasses (speckled with raindrops), wearing a polo shirt that is completely soaked through — the fabric darkened and clinging. He faces the viewer with mild exasperation, not distress. He is wet. He is calm. Behind him, the front door is closed. Next to the door, a large living room window glows with warm amber light from inside — cozy, inviting, dry. Through the window, a vague silhouette of a woman is visible — she appears to be seated on a couch, reading or watching TV, warm and comfortable. Noah has not been let in. He does not appear to be panicking about this.

Scene 2 (2.2–5.2s): SETUP — The Observation
Same exterior, same rain. Noah Check on the front step (soaked polo shirt, wet hair, rain-speckled glasses) faces the viewer. One hand makes a small resigned gesture toward the window behind him — indicating his wife inside. His expression is factual, mildly puzzled — he is presenting data about the situation. He has been told his behavior is weird. He is processing this assessment. Rain runs down his face and drips from his chin. He does not wipe it away. Through the window behind him, the warm silhouette of the woman is still visible — she has not moved, has not acknowledged the man standing in the rain outside her window. The contrast between the warm, dry interior and the cold, wet exterior is absolute.
Caption: "My wife thinks it's weird that I stare through the window during a rainstorm."

Scene 3 (5.2–5.6s): THE BEAT
Same exterior, closer framing on Noah. Rain on his glasses. Water dripping from his collar. His expression shifts from puzzled to problem-solving — he has identified the fix. It is obvious. He is going to share it.

Scene 4 (5.6–8.6s): PUNCHLINE — The Solution
Same exterior, same rain. Noah Check on the front step (soaked, wet hair, glasses, polo shirt) looks at the viewer with genuine problem-solving energy. His hands spread slightly — palms up, the universal "it's simple" gesture. Rain pools in his upturned palms. He has identified the variable that makes the situation weird, and it is not him staring through the window. It is the fact that he is on the wrong side of it. Behind him, the window still glows warm. The silhouette inside has not moved. The door is still closed. Noah is not angry. He is not pleading. He is presenting a logical solution to a perception problem. He would like to come inside. He does not consider this an unreasonable request. The rain continues.
Caption: "It would be less weird if she'd just let me in."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.2s no text | 2.2–5.2s setup | 5.6–8.6s punchline
Voice: Noah mild puzzlement, factual. 0.4s pause. Punchline delivered as genuine problem-solving — he has identified the fix and is sharing it. No pleading, no anger. Just data.
Risk: LOW. Rain effect in cartoon style is manageable. Fallback: remove rain particles, just show Noah in wet clothes outside a lit window — the wetness implies the rain.
Jerry hook: "The visual contrast — soaking wet man calmly explaining himself while warm light glows behind him — is instantly funny before the first word. The silhouette wife not moving is the second joke."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 8.6s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline + rain + window glow, Scene 1 establish wet Noah + warm window], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

---

**Concept B — "The Window POV"**
Style: Interior looking out — the viewer sees Noah through the window from the wife's perspective first, then cuts outside
Duration: 9.2s — Extra time for the perspective shift.

Master video prompt:
"Two perspectives: interior warm living room looking through rain-streaked window at Noah's face outside, then exterior showing Noah in rain with house behind him. Cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.5s): HOOK — The View from Inside
A warm, cozy living room at night. A comfortable couch with a blanket, a reading lamp casting amber light, a mug of tea on a side table. The room is inviting and dry. But filling the large window on the far wall is a face — Noah Check's face, pressed close to the glass from outside. Short cropped hair plastered flat with rain. Rectangular glasses fogged and speckled with water droplets. His expression through the rain-streaked glass is mild, patient, and slightly forlorn — not scary, just... there. Watching. The warm interior and the face in the rain-streaked window create an image that is simultaneously unsettling and absurd. Rain streams down the glass around his features. He has been there for a while.

Scene 2 (2.5–5.5s): SETUP — His Side
The perspective has shifted to exterior. The suburban house at night, heavy rain. Noah Check stands outside the window — full body now visible. Short cropped hair soaked flat, rectangular glasses rain-speckled, polo shirt completely drenched and clinging. He faces the viewer (away from the window now). His expression is mild exasperation. One hand gestures behind him toward the window — through which the warm interior is now visible from outside, the couch, the lamp, the tea. A vague female silhouette is seated inside. He is explaining the situation. He is standing in the rain in front of his own house. He sees the issue. The issue is not him.
Caption: "My wife thinks it's weird that I stare through the window during a rainstorm."

Scene 3 (5.5–5.9s): THE BEAT
Same exterior. Closer on Noah. Rain running down his face. He does not wipe it. His expression shifts to rational clarity — the solution is obvious. He blinks rain out of his eyes.

Scene 4 (5.9–9.2s): PUNCHLINE — The Logic
Same exterior, rain. Noah Check (soaked polo, wet hair, speckled glasses) looks at the viewer with genuine, calm problem-solving. His hands make a small conclusive gesture. He has run the analysis. The weird variable is not the staring. The weird variable is his location relative to the window. If she let him in, he would be looking out the window, which is normal. He is currently looking in the window, which is weird. He has identified this. He has proposed the solution. He is waiting for implementation. Behind him, the warm window glow persists. The silhouette inside has not moved. Rain pools at Noah's feet. He is patient. He is rational. He is very, very wet.
Caption: "It would be less weird if she'd just let me in."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.5s no text | 2.5–5.5s setup | 5.9–9.2s punchline
Voice: Same flat Noah delivery. 0.4s pause. Punchline as logical conclusion.
Risk: LOW. The interior-to-exterior shift is a two-location cut but both are simple. Fallback: skip the interior POV, use Concept A's single-exterior approach.
Jerry hook: "Opening on Noah's face in the rain-streaked window FROM INSIDE is a horror movie setup that becomes a dad joke. The genre bait-and-switch hooks instantly. The audience expects a thriller and gets a man who wants to come inside."
Vince handoff: 9:16, 9.2s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 1 face-in-window interior POV, Scene 4 exterior punchline], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

---

quality_gate: PASS
status: ready_for_jerry_review
